items.q: What is the difference between profit and revenue?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity sold); profit is revenue minus total costs — what is left after paying for everything.
items.misconception: Profit and revenue are different words for the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Profit is what businesses earn; revenue is what employees earn.
items.why: A business can have huge revenue but make no profit if costs are equally huge. Conflating them leads to nonsensical analysis — Amazon had high revenue but low profits for years.

items.correct: An arrangement where a franchisor grants a franchisee the right to use their brand, products and systems in exchange for fees and a share of revenue.
items.misconception: A franchise is when a business owns multiple branches itself.
items.random_wrong: A franchise is when employees buy shares in their company.
items.why: Owned chains are not franchises. A franchise involves separate ownership — McDonald's restaurants are mostly franchised (owned by individual operators), not owned by McDonald's Corporation.

items.q: What is Herzberg's distinction between hygiene factors and motivators?
items.correct: Hygiene factors (pay, conditions, policies) prevent dissatisfaction when present but don't motivate; motivators (recognition, achievement, responsibility) create positive satisfaction.
items.random_wrong: Hygiene factors are about workplace cleanliness.
items.why: Pay is a hygiene factor — its absence demotivates but its presence doesn't motivate beyond a point. Students treat pay as the prime motivator; Herzberg's theory directly challenges this.

items.q: Clayton Christensen's 'The Innovator's Dilemma' was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1997. “1985” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Number of steps in Kotter's change management model?
items.why: The real figure is 8 steps. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
